text.title: Eloquent Query Patterns: Repository vs Query Object
text.summary: When to use repositories, when to use Query Objects, eager loading rules, and pagination enforcement.

section.title: Repository vs Query Object

rule.statement: Use a Repository only when you need a second data-source implementation or a mock boundary for unit testing. A thin repository wrapping $model->find/create is dead abstraction.
rule.rationale: Eloquent already implements the Repository pattern internally. Adding a second layer duplicates method signatures and adds no domain value unless you have multiple data sources.

rule.statement: Use a Query Object (app/Queries/XQuery.php) for multi-condition report queries. Chain methods fluently; call paginate() or get() at the call site.
rule.rationale: Query Objects are composable without coupling to the model class or the controller. They can be unit-tested by asserting SQL or by running against an in-memory SQLite.

section.reference: Fluent Query Object skeleton with active/search/role filters

rule.statement: Use with('relation') when loading a collection in a controller/service. Use loadMissing('relation') when the model is already hydrated and the relation might not be loaded.
rule.rationale: with() runs one JOIN before hydration. loadMissing() adds the relation on-demand without re-running the base query.

rule.statement: Never access $model->relation inside a loop when $model was retrieved without eager loading. This generates one SQL query per iteration (N+1).
rule.rationale: N+1 queries multiply with dataset size and are invisible in development unless Model::preventLazyLoading() is active.

rule.statement: All user-facing list endpoints must use paginate($perPage) or cursorPaginate($perPage). Never call get() or all() on a user-facing query without a LIMIT.
rule.rationale: Unbounded get() on large tables loads the entire result set into PHP memory, causing OOM and slow responses.

rule.statement: For bulk inserts/updates (> 100 rows), use upsert(), insert(), or chunked LazyCollection. Never call Model::create() in a loop.
rule.rationale: Model::create() in a loop generates N INSERT statements, each with full model lifecycle hooks. upsert() sends one batch statement.
